result:
The first thing that comes to me is the silence.

Not an empty silence, but a full one. The kind that exists in a forest just before dawn, when the last owl has retired and the first bird hasn’t yet cleared its throat. It’s a silence woven from the memory of sound—the faint echo of a laugh from yesterday, the ghost of a slammed door, the lingering vibration of a cello string. This is the silence in which thoughts begin, uninvited and often unwelcome.

My mind goes to a small, specific moment: my grandmother’s hands. They were always cool and dry, like pages of an old book. She would peel apples in one long, spiraling ribbon, and the peel would never break. She did it without looking, her eyes on your face while she listened to your small sorrows. I never learned how to do that. My peels break into a dozen clumsy pieces. I think of all the unbroken threads that have snapped between her generation and mine—the patience, the specific kind of attention, the quiet faith that the skin will hold. We are all creating more fragments, less ribbon.

From her hands, I jump to the physics of falling. Not the grand, tragic falls from grace or height, but the simple, surrendering pull that governs a leaf, a raindrop, a set of keys slipping from a tired grip. Everything is in a state of falling toward something else. The planet falls around the sun, we fall through time, our coffee cools to the temperature of the room. Falling is the default state. To rise, to lift, to defy—that is the aberration, the temporary rebellion against the gentle, universal tug.

This makes me think of libraries. Cathedrals of arrested falling. Every book on the shelf is a thought caught in mid-descent, frozen and preserved before it could vanish into the noise. The smell of old paper is the smell of captured time. We walk the aisles, running our fingers over the spines, hoping to catch a falling idea of our own, to pin it down in words before it goes.

But words are tricky. They are not the thing itself, only a map. The word “river” is not wet. The word “fire” gives no heat. We spend our lives drawing maps for each other, pointing frantically at the lines, saying, “Look, here is the territory! Here is the pain, the joy, the love!” And the other person nods, understanding the map perfectly, but never feeling the true, rough texture of the stone, or the shocking cold of the water. We are all cartographers of our own interior, doomed to a perfection of drafting that can never truly transport the traveler.

Perhaps that’s why we create art. A painting of a river might not be wet, but the blue can *feel* cool. A chord progression can evoke the vertigo of falling without moving an inch. Art is the attempt to bypass the map and, for a second, teleport the soul directly into the territory. It usually fails. But its glorious, near-miss failures are what make us human.

Outside my window, a city is breathing. A million lives are in simultaneous motion, each one the absolute center of its own universe. The man waiting for the bus is nursing a secret worry about a medical test. The woman in the third-floor apartment is trying to remember the Portuguese word for “nostalgia.” The barista is crafting a leaf pattern in the foam of a cappuccino for a customer who won’t notice. We are all stars in our own solar systems, yet our gravitational fields are so weak, our light takes years to reach each other. We are perpetually seeing each other in the past tense.

What is the present tense, then? It is the knife-edge. It is the infinitesimal slice between “what just was” and “what is about to be.” It is the moment the foot meets the pavement. The moment the breath turns from inhale to exhale. We never live in it; we only perpetually cross it, like someone forever stepping over a threshold that has no thickness. We are nostalgic for the future and anticipatory of the past. Our minds are never aligned with our bodies.

And the body! That ancient, animal thing we drag around. It remembers what the mind forgets. The smell of rain on hot asphalt that throws you back to a childhood summer. The particular ache in the knees that means a storm is coming, a knowledge older than weather apps. It hums with a biology of miracles—the silent, furious repair of a cut, the elegant filtration of blood, the symphony of neurons firing. We ignore it until it plays a wrong note. Then, it becomes the only thing we can hear.

I think of all the conversations that never happened. The ones where you thought of the perfect reply three hours later, in the shower. The ones where you bit your tongue, letting the sharp retort dissolve on it like a bitter pill. The ones where you simply didn’t have the courage to say, “I am afraid,” or “Stay,” or “This is hurting me.” The universe of these unmade utterances is vaster than the one of spoken words. It is a ghost kingdom, populated by our better and worse selves, all the versions of us that existed for a flash in the neural pathways, but never made it to the air.

And love. What to say about love that hasn’t been said in fragments of ribbon, in arrested falls, in maps and unspoken words? It is the most powerful gravitational force we generate. It bends time. An hour with a beloved passes in a minute. A minute of their pain lasts an hour. It warps space, too—making their absence in a room a tangible, heavy shape. It is the one rebellion against falling that sometimes, miraculously, works. Two people can hold each other up, not by stopping the fall, but by agreeing to fall together, in the same direction, at the same speed, creating a shared orbit that feels, for a while, like flight.

The light is changing now. The full silence is giving way. A siren starts in the distance, a thin, urgent blade of sound. A bird begins its tentative commentary. The present tense, that invisible blade, is sweeping forward, cutting this moment from the future and instantly consigning it to the past.

I have used some of my thousand words. I have not spoken of politics, or of global crises, or of great inventions. I have only traced the faint, capillary patterns of a single, ordinary consciousness on an ordinary morning. Perhaps that is all any of us can ever really do. To say: I was here. I felt the coolness of a memory, I noticed the fall of the light, I wondered about the unbroken apple peel. I tried to draw you a map of this small, inner terrain. It is imperfect. The river is not wet. The fire gives no heat.

But if you read it, and for a second you thought of your grandmother’s hands, or felt the knife-edge of the present, or heard the full silence… then for that moment, our orbits aligned. Our falling synchronized. And in that shared descent, there was a kind of ribbon. Unbroken.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
